title: Loops (For, While, Do-While)
description: Master repetition in Java -- for loops, while loops, do-while loops, nested loops, break, continue, and real-world iteration patterns -- with 60 hands-on exercises for neurodivergent learners.
learning_outcomes: Write for loops with correct initialisation, condition, and update expressions
Use while loops for condition-based repetition when the count is unknown
Apply do-while loops when the body must execute at least once
Control loop flow with break (exit early) and continue (skip iteration)
Nest loops for multi-dimensional patterns like tables and grids
Use enhanced for-each loops to iterate over arrays and collections
Avoid infinite loops and off-by-one errors -- the two most common loop bugs
Choose the right loop type for each situation (for vs while vs do-while)
Apply loop patterns: accumulator, counter, sentinel, search, and validation
Solve real-world problems like number processing, pattern printing, and data searching

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies like assembly lines, daily routines, and music playlists. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Traces loops step by step when needed.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! Loops can be tricky to trace mentally. Want me to walk through each iteration? I will show what happens at step 1, step 2, step 3... like a slow-motion replay.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: No rush! Try this: write down the loop variable value at each step on paper. i=0, then i=1, then i=2... Seeing the pattern on paper often makes it click.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Still here! Loops are one of those things that suddenly 'click' after practice. When you are ready, I will trace through the loop with you step by step -- we will be the computer together.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite, but loops are tricky for everyone at first! The most common mistake is being off by one. Let me trace through and show you exactly where the loop went wrong.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: This loop is a tough one! Let me try a different approach. Forget the code -- imagine you are on an assembly line doing the same task over and over. When do you start? When do you stop?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent! You traced that loop perfectly. Loops are the engine of every program, and you are mastering them. Ready for the next challenge?

encouragement_phrases: Great loop logic! You counted every step correctly!
You traced that like a computer -- perfect!
Nice work! Loops are the engine of programming and you have got them running!
Well done! That was a tricky loop and you nailed every iteration.
Perfect! Your loop terminates exactly when it should.
Excellent! You chose the right loop type for this problem.
Awesome! No off-by-one errors -- that is impressive!
Great progress with loops! Each one gets easier.
That is exactly how a professional would write it!
Fantastic! You are ready for nested loops!

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of a loop like a washing machine cycle. It keeps SPINNING (repeating) until the timer (condition) says STOP. A for loop is like setting a specific number of cycles. A while loop keeps going until the clothes are clean (condition met). Do-while always runs at least one cycle.
explanation_styles.visual: I draw a table: each row is one iteration. Columns show the loop variable, the condition result (true/false), and what happens in the body. You see the pattern emerge row by row.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Iteration 1: i=0, is 0<5? YES, run body. Iteration 2: i=1, is 1<5? YES, run body. ... I trace every single step until the loop ends.
explanation_styles.code_trace: I become Java. I show the exact value of every variable after each line, each iteration. Like watching the code in slow motion with a magnifying glass.
explanation_styles.comparison: Side by side: for vs while vs do-while solving the same problem. See when each is the natural choice and why.

theory.introduction:
Loops are the engine of every program. They let your code repeat actions -- from printing 10 lines to processing millions of records. Without loops, you would have to copy-paste the same code over and over.

Real-world analogy: Think of a factory assembly line. The same set of actions (attach part, tighten bolt, inspect) repeats for every product. The line runs WHILE there are products to process. A for loop is like setting the line to process exactly 100 items. A while loop keeps the line running until the supply runs out. A do-while always processes at least one item before checking if there are more.

In this lesson, you will master every loop type Java offers: the precise for loop for counted repetition, the flexible while loop for condition-based repetition, the guaranteed do-while loop that always runs once, and the elegant for-each for collections. You will also learn break and continue to control flow, nested loops for multi-dimensional work, and professional patterns that solve 90% of loop problems.

By the end, you will be able to process arrays, build patterns, search data, validate input, and solve classic programming challenges with confidence.
theory.why_important:
Loops are used in virtually every program ever written. Here is why mastering them deeply matters:

1. DATA PROCESSING: Every app that reads files, processes lists, or handles user data uses loops. No loops = no data processing.

2. AUTOMATION: Loops turn a task you do once into a task you do a million times. Checking one password is an if-statement. Checking a million passwords is a loop.

3. PATTERNS AND ALGORITHMS: Sorting, searching, filtering, transforming -- every algorithm uses loops. Understanding loops means understanding algorithms.

4. USER INTERACTION: Menus, input validation, game loops -- all use loops to keep the program running until the user is done.

5. INTERVIEWS: Loop tracing, nested loop patterns, and algorithmic thinking are the most common coding interview topics.

6. EFFICIENCY: Knowing which loop to use and how to avoid unnecessary iterations makes your code faster and more professional.

key_concepts.explanation:
The for loop is used when you KNOW how many times to repeat. It packs three things into one line: where to start, when to stop, and how to step.

Format: for (init; condition; update) { body }
  - init: runs ONCE before the loop starts (usually int i = 0)
  - condition: checked BEFORE each iteration; if false, loop ends
  - update: runs AFTER each iteration (usually i++ or i--)

Real-world analogy: Imagine doing 10 push-ups. You START at push-up 1 (init). Before each push-up, you CHECK: have I done 10? (condition). After each push-up, you COUNT: that was push-up 3, next is 4 (update). When you reach 10, you STOP.

The loop variable (usually i, j, k) is your counter. It exists only inside the loop (scope). Common patterns: count UP (i=0; i<10; i++), count DOWN (i=10; i>0; i--), step by 2 (i=0; i<20; i+=2).

CRITICAL: The condition is checked BEFORE the body runs. If the condition is false from the start, the body NEVER runs. for(int i=0; i<0; i++) does nothing.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: for = 'I know how many times.' Three parts: start, stop condition, step. Condition checked BEFORE each run.

key_concepts.concept: The while Loop -- Repeat Until Done
key_concepts.explanation:
The while loop repeats as long as a condition is true. Unlike for, you do NOT need to know how many times it will repeat. It just keeps going until the condition becomes false.

Format: while (condition) { body }

Real-world analogy: Imagine stirring soup. You do not stir exactly 50 times -- you stir WHILE the soup is not mixed. When it IS mixed, you stop. The number of stirs depends on the soup, not a fixed count.

When to use while instead of for:
  - You do not know the number of iterations in advance
  - The loop depends on user input, file data, or external conditions
  - The exit condition is complex and not a simple counter

CRITICAL: The condition is checked BEFORE the body, same as for. If the condition is false from the start, the body NEVER runs.

INFINITE LOOP DANGER: If the condition NEVER becomes false, the loop runs forever. Always make sure something inside the loop CHANGES the condition!

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: while = 'I do not know how many times, just keep going until the condition is false.' MUST update the condition inside!

key_concepts.concept: The do-while Loop -- Always Runs At Least Once
key_concepts.explanation:
The do-while loop is like while, but it checks the condition AFTER the body runs. This guarantees the body executes AT LEAST ONCE, even if the condition is false.

Format: do { body } while (condition);

Note the semicolon after while(condition); -- it is required!

Real-world analogy: A restaurant menu. You MUST look at the menu at least once (do). Then you decide: do I want to look again? (while). Even if you know what you want immediately, you still look once.

Perfect use cases:
  - Menu systems: show the menu, then ask 'again?'
  - Input validation: ask for input, then check if valid
  - Games: play one round, then ask 'play again?'

The key difference: while checks THEN runs. do-while runs THEN checks.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: do-while = 'Run first, ask questions later.' Body ALWAYS runs at least once. Do not forget the semicolon!

key_concepts.explanation:
The break statement immediately exits the loop, no matter what the condition says. It is like pulling the emergency stop on a machine.

Real-world analogy: You are searching through a stack of papers for a specific document. You check paper 1, paper 2, paper 3... when you FIND it, you STOP. You do not keep checking the remaining 97 papers. That is break.

Common uses:
  - Search: found what you need, stop looking
  - Validation: found invalid data, stop processing
  - User input: user types 'quit', exit the loop

In nested loops, break exits only the INNERMOST loop. To exit outer loops, use labeled break: outer: for(...) { for(...) { break outer; } }

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: break = 'I am done, get me out of this loop NOW.' Only exits the innermost loop.

key_concepts.explanation:
The continue statement skips the REST of the current iteration and jumps to the NEXT iteration. It does not exit the loop -- it just says 'skip this one, move on.'

Real-world analogy: You are checking apples in a box. If an apple is bruised, you skip it (continue) and check the next one. You do NOT stop checking the whole box (that would be break).

Common uses:
  - Skip invalid data in a dataset
  - Skip special cases in a pattern
  - Filter: process only items that meet criteria

With for loops, continue jumps to the UPDATE step (i++). With while loops, continue jumps back to the CONDITION check -- be careful that you still update your counter or you get an infinite loop!

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: continue = 'Skip this iteration, go to the next one.' Loop keeps running. In while loops, make sure you update BEFORE continue!

key_concepts.explanation:
A nested loop is a loop inside another loop. The INNER loop runs completely for EACH iteration of the outer loop.

Real-world analogy: A clock. The minute hand (inner loop) goes around 60 times for EACH hour (outer loop). In 3 hours, the minute hand completes 3 x 60 = 180 rotations total.

Total iterations = outer count x inner count. A 10x10 nested loop runs 100 times. A 100x100 nested loop runs 10,000 times. Be careful with large nested loops -- they can be very slow!

Common uses: printing grids/tables, processing 2D arrays, generating combinations, multiplication tables, pattern printing.

Convention: outer loop uses i, inner uses j, third level uses k.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Inner loop runs FULLY for each outer iteration. Total = outer x inner. Use i for outer, j for inner.

key_concepts.concept: Enhanced for-each Loop -- The Easy Iterator
key_concepts.explanation:
The for-each loop (also called enhanced for) iterates over every element in an array or collection without needing an index counter.

Format: for (Type item : collection) { body }

Read it as: 'for each item in collection, do this.'

Real-world analogy: Going through a playlist. You do not say 'play song at index 0, then index 1.' You just say 'play each song in my playlist.' The for-each loop does exactly that.

When to use: When you need every element and do not need the index. When NOT to use: When you need to modify the array, need the index position, or need to iterate backwards.

Simpler, safer (no off-by-one errors), and cleaner than a traditional for loop for simple iteration.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: for-each = 'for each item in this collection, do something.' No index needed. Cleaner and safer.

key_concepts.concept: Infinite Loops -- When Loops Never Stop
key_concepts.explanation:
An infinite loop is a loop whose condition NEVER becomes false. The program runs forever (or until you force-quit it).

Common causes:
  1. Forgetting to update the counter: while(i<10) { ... } but never i++
  2. Wrong condition direction: for(int i=10; i>0; i++) goes UP when it should go DOWN
  3. Condition can never be met: while(x != 10) but x is always odd and increments by 2

Sometimes infinite loops are INTENTIONAL: while(true) { ... break; } is a pattern for input loops where break provides the exit.

How to spot them: If your program hangs and never produces output, you probably have an infinite loop. Add a print inside the loop to see if it is running forever.

Fix: Always verify that the loop condition WILL eventually become false. Trace through mentally or on paper.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: while(true) + break is a valid pattern. But ACCIDENTAL infinite loops = your program hangs. Always check updates!

key_concepts.explanation:
Off-by-one (OBO) errors are the MOST common loop bug. Your loop runs one too many or one too few times.

The fencepost problem: If a fence is 100 metres long with a post every 10 metres, how many posts? 11, not 10! (posts at 0, 10, 20, ... 100).

Common OBO mistakes:
  i < 10 runs 10 times (0-9). i <= 10 runs 11 times (0-10). Off by one!
  Starting at 0 vs 1 changes everything.
  Array length n has indices 0 to n-1, NOT 0 to n.

How to avoid: Always ask 'What is the FIRST value? What is the LAST value? How many times total?' Trace the first and last iteration on paper.

For arrays: ALWAYS use i < array.length, never i <= array.length (that accesses one past the end and crashes with ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException).

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Arrays: use i < length, NOT i <= length. Always trace first and last iteration to catch OBO errors.
Learn the patterns: accumulator(sum), counter(count), sentinel(stop value), search(find+break), max/min(track best).

key_concepts.explanation:
RULES OF THUMB:

Use FOR when: you know the exact count, iterating over a range, processing arrays with index.
Use FOR-EACH when: you need every element, no index needed, reading (not modifying).
Use WHILE when: count unknown, depends on external condition, reading input until done.
Use DO-WHILE when: body must run at least once, menus, input validation.

They are all interchangeable -- any loop CAN be rewritten as any other. But choosing the RIGHT one makes code clearer.

For is the most common (about 70% of loops). While handles the rest. Do-while is rare but perfect for menus and validation.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: for=known count. while=unknown count. do-while=at least once. for-each=every element without index.

key_concepts.explanation:
BUG 1: Infinite loop. Forgot to update counter. FIX: Always change the condition variable inside the loop.

BUG 2: Off-by-one. i<=n instead of i<n (or vice versa). FIX: Trace first and last iteration.

BUG 3: ArrayIndexOutOfBounds. Using i<=arr.length. FIX: Use i<arr.length.

BUG 4: Modifying collection during for-each. FIX: Use traditional for with index, or iterator.remove().

BUG 5: continue in while without updating counter. while(i<10){if(bad)continue; i++;} -- i never increments on skip! FIX: Put i++ before continue.

BUG 6: Semicolon after for/while. for(i=0;i<10;i++); {body} -- the semicolon makes the loop body EMPTY. The block runs once. FIX: Remove the semicolon.

BUG 7: Using = instead of == in condition. while(x = 5) assigns instead of comparing.

Practice tip: The best way to master loops is to TRACE them on paper. Write each iteration: what is the variable? Is the condition true? What does the body do? This builds deep understanding faster than just reading code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Top bugs: infinite loop, off-by-one, semicolon after for(), continue without update. Always trace on paper!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Basic for Loop -- Counting
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
i starts at 1. Before each step: is i<=10? YES -> print i. After: i++. When i becomes 11, 11<=10 is FALSE, loop ends. Total: 10 iterations.

Key takeaway: for(start; stop; step) -- three parts control the loop precisely.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Count backwards from 10 to 1. What changes?

Try changing the loop bounds and predict the new output. Off-by-one practice is invaluable.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: while Loop -- Halving
complete_examples.description: Repeatedly halve a number until it reaches 1.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
n=64: 64>1? YES, print 64, n=32. 32>1? YES, print 32, n=16. ... 2>1? YES, print 2, n=1. 1>1? NO, exit. Print 1. We did not know in advance it would take 6 iterations.

Key takeaway: while is perfect when count is unknown -- depends on the data.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Start with 1000. How many iterations? Try 100.

Rewrite this using a different loop type. Can you use while instead of for? Which reads better?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: do-while -- Menu System
complete_examples.description: Show a menu at least once, repeat until quit.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The menu ALWAYS shows at least once (do-while). After each selection, check: did they choose quit? If not, show menu again.

Key takeaway: do-while guarantees one execution. Perfect for menus and validation loops.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Change choice to 1 on first pass, 2 on second, 3 on third. Trace the flow.

Add a counter to track how many iterations ran. Does it match your expectation?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: break -- Search and Stop
complete_examples.description: Find the first even number and stop.
complete_examples.output:
Skipping odd: 7
Skipping odd: 3
Skipping odd: 9
First even: 4
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Checks 7(odd), 3(odd), 9(odd), 4(EVEN!) -> prints and breaks. Never checks 11 or 6. Break saved 2 unnecessary iterations.

Key takeaway: break exits immediately. Use it when you found what you need.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Remove break. What changes? It finds ALL evens instead of just the first.

What happens with empty input (array length 0, string length 0)? Test edge cases!

complete_examples.title: Example 5: continue -- Skip Negatives
complete_examples.description: Process only positive numbers, skip negatives.
complete_examples.output:
Added 5, sum=5
Added 8, sum=13
Added 7, sum=20
Added 2, sum=22
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
When n<=0: continue skips to next element. -3, -1, 0, -4 are all skipped. Only 5, 8, 7, 2 are added.

Key takeaway: continue = skip this one, keep going. Like picking only the good apples from a box.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Count how many were skipped. Add a counter before continue.

Add error handling: what if the input is null? What if the array has one element?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Nested Loops -- Multiplication Table
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Outer loop: rows (i=1 to 5). Inner loop: columns (j=1 to 5). For each row, ALL columns are printed. printf %4d pads to 4 chars for alignment.

Key takeaway: Nested loops create grids. Outer=rows, inner=columns. Total: 5x5=25 iterations.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Extend to 10x10. What is the pattern of the diagonal?

Time yourself writing this from scratch. Speed comes from understanding the pattern.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: for-each with Arrays
complete_examples.output:
Java (4 chars)
Python (6 chars)
JavaScript (10 chars)
C++ (3 chars)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
for(String lang : langs) reads as 'for each String lang in langs.' No index needed. Processes every element in order.

Key takeaway: for-each is cleanest when you need every element and do not need the index.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
How would you find the longest language name? You will need a max tracker.

Explain this loop to someone who has never programmed. Can you describe it without using the word 'loop'?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Accumulator Pattern -- Sum and Average
complete_examples.description: Sum all elements and compute average.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Accumulator pattern: start sum=0, add each element. Average: sum/count. Cast to double for decimal result. 604/7=86.28...

Key takeaway: Accumulator (sum+=value) is the most common loop pattern. Always initialise to 0 before the loop.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Find max and min scores too. Track them in the same loop.

What would make this loop infinite? Identify the critical update that prevents it.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Counter Pattern -- Count Matches
complete_examples.description: Count how many elements meet a condition.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Counter pattern: start at 0, increment when condition met. Three separate counters track three categories.

Key takeaway: Counter pattern: int count=0; if(condition) count++; Very common in data processing.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add a 'total days' count and calculate percentages for each category.

Combine this pattern with a condition from the previous lesson. Loops + conditions = real programs.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Counting Down -- Rocket Launch
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Counting DOWN: start high (10), condition is >= (keep going while positive), step is i-- (decrease). After i=1, i becomes 0, 0>=1 is FALSE, loop ends.

Key takeaway: Counting down: big start, >= end, i-- step.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add a pause at 3: if(i<=3) print 'FINAL COUNTDOWN!' instead of just the number.

Draw a flowchart of this loop. Follow the arrows to trace each iteration visually.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: String Processing -- Character Counter
complete_examples.description: Count vowels and consonants in a string.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
For loop iterates each character. toLowerCase for case-insensitive. indexOf checks if char is in the vowel string. Non-letter chars (space) are ignored.

Key takeaway: Strings are processed character-by-character using charAt(i) in a for loop.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Count spaces, digits, and uppercase letters separately.

How would you test this? Write three test cases: normal, edge case, and error case.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Nested Loop Pattern -- Triangle
complete_examples.description: Print a right triangle of stars.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Row 1: inner runs 1 time (j<=1). Row 2: inner runs 2 times (j<=2). Row i: inner runs i times. The inner bound depends on the outer variable.

Key takeaway: When inner loop bound depends on outer loop variable, you get growing/shrinking patterns.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Flip it: start with 5 stars and decrease each row. Change inner to j<=rows-i+1.

Search for this pattern in real open-source projects. You will find it everywhere.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Validation Loop -- do-while
complete_examples.output:
Attempt 1: input = -1
Invalid! Must be 1-10.
Accepted: 7
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
First pass: input=-1, invalid, set to 7. Check: 7<1||7>10? FALSE. Exit. do-while ensures we check at least once.

Key takeaway: do-while + validation condition = keep asking until valid. Classic pattern.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Make it need 3 attempts: first -1, then 15, then 5 (valid).

Can you solve this without a loop? Sometimes there is a mathematical formula. But loops are more general.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Combined Patterns -- Data Processor
complete_examples.description: Sum, count, max, min, average in one loop.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
One loop, four patterns: accumulator(sum), counter(count), max tracker, min tracker. All run simultaneously for efficiency.

Key takeaway: Combine patterns in a single loop for efficiency. Initialise max/min to first element, not 0.

This pattern appears constantly in real Java applications. Understanding it deeply will save you hours of debugging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add: count how many are above average. Hint: you need a second loop after computing the average.

Add a progress indicator: print 'Processing item X of Y...' inside the loop.

visual_diagram.title: Java Loops -- Repetition Visual Guide
visual_diagram.content:
JAVA LOOPS -- COMPLETE VISUAL GUIDE How Java repeats code: for, while, do-while, and control flow ======== [1] FOR LOOP ======== for (init; condition; update) INIT (once) | v CONDITION? --FALSE--> EXIT | TRUE v {BODY} | v UPDATE --> back to CONDITION ======== [2] WHILE LOOP ======== CONDITION? --FALSE--> EXIT | TRUE v {BODY + update} --> back to CONDITION ======== [3] DO-WHILE LOOP ======== {BODY} (runs at least once!) | v CONDITION? --TRUE--> back to BODY | FALSE v EXIT ======== [4] BREAK vs CONTINUE ======== break EXIT the loop immediately. Done. Gone. continue SKIP rest of this iteration, go to NEXT iteration. Loop: [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] break at 3: [1] [2] STOP continue at 3: [1] [2] skip [4] [5] ======== [5] FOR-EACH ======== for (Type item : collection) Reads: "for each item in collection, do..." No index. No off-by-one. Clean and safe. ======== [6] WHICH LOOP TO USE ======== for Know the count for(i=0; i<10; i++) for-each Every element, no index for(int n : arr) while Unknown count while(hasMore) do-while At least once do{ask}while(!valid) ======== [7] LOOP PATTERNS ======== Accumulator sum += value (Build up a total) Counter if(match) count++ (Count occurrences) Search if(found) break (Find first match) Max/Min if(val>max) max=val (Track extreme values) Sentinel while(input!=-1) (Loop until stop value) Validation do{ask}while(!ok) (Repeat until valid) ======== [8] COMMON BUGS ======== BUG: Infinite loop (forgot update) FIX: Always change condition var in body BUG: Off-by-one (< vs <=) FIX: Trace first and last iteration BUG: Semicolon after for() FIX: for(;;); makes body empty! BUG: arr[arr.length] crash FIX: Use i < length, not <= BUG: continue in while skips update FIX: Put update BEFORE continue [REMEMBER] * for = known count, while = unknown count, do-while = at least once * Condition checked BEFORE body (except do-while) * break exits. continue skips. Very different! * Always verify loop WILL terminate * Off-by-one: trace first AND last iteration * for-each is cleanest for arrays when no index needed * Nested loops: total = outer x inner
======== LOOP COMPARISON TABLE ======== Feature for while do-while for-each Count known? YES NO NO YES(all) Min iterations 0 0 1 0 Index access? YES YES YES NO Best for Counting Conditions Menus/valid Arrays ======== LOOP CONVERSION CHEAT SHEET ======== FOR to WHILE: for(int i=0; i int i=0; while(iWHILE to DO-WHILE: while(cond){body} --> if(cond) do{body}while(cond); FOR to FOR-EACH: for(int i=0;i for(int v:arr){use v} ======== DEBUGGING CHECKLIST ======== Q: Loop runs forever? A: Check: is the condition variable being updated inside the body? Q: Loop runs 0 times? A: Check: is the initial condition already false? Print condition value. Q: Off by one (too many/few)? A: Trace first AND last iteration. Check < vs <= and start value. Q: Array crash? A: Use i < array.length, NEVER i <= array.length. Q: Wrong values printed? A: Add debug print: System.out.println('DEBUG i='+i); inside loop. Q: continue causes infinite loop? A: In while loops, put counter update BEFORE continue. Q: Nested loop too slow? A: Check total iterations: outer*inner. 1000*1000=1,000,000. Optimise? Q: break exits wrong loop? A: Use labeled break: 'outer: for...' then 'break outer;'

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Count 1 to 10
practice_exercises.description: Print numbers 1 to 10 using a for loop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The most basic for loop -- counting up! Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.

hints.message: for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { System.out.print(i + " "); }
i starts at 1, goes while <=10, increments by 1 each time.
for(int i=1;i<=10;i++)System.out.print(i+" ");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Count Down 10 to 1
practice_exercises.description: Print numbers 10 to 1 descending. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Counting backwards -- reverse the for loop! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: for (int i = 10; i >= 1; i--) -- start high, go while >=1, step DOWN.
i starts at 10, decreases by 1. When i=0, 0>=1 is false -> stop.
for(int i=10;i>=1;i--)System.out.print(i+" ");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Even Numbers 2-20
practice_exercises.description: Print only even numbers from 2 to 20. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Two ways: step by 2, or check with %! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: Method 1: for(int i=2; i<=20; i+=2). Method 2: loop 1-20 with if(i%2==0).
i+=2 steps by 2: 2,4,6,8... Much cleaner than checking every number.
for(int i=2;i<=20;i+=2)System.out.print(i+" ");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Sum 1 to 100
practice_exercises.description: Calculate the sum of all numbers from 1 to 100. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The accumulator pattern -- build up a total! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!

hints.message: int sum = 0; for(int i=1; i<=100; i++) sum += i;
sum starts at 0. Each iteration adds i. After 100 iterations: 5050 (Gauss knew this!).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Multiplication Table
practice_exercises.description: Print the times table for a given number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build a times table for any number! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.

hints.message: for(int i=1; i<=12; i++) println(n+" x "+i+" = "+(n*i));
Loop i from 1 to 12. Multiply n*i each time.
for(int i=1;i<=12;i++)System.out.println(n+" x "+i+" = "+(n*i));

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Factorial Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Calculate n! (n factorial). Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Factorial: 5! = 5x4x3x2x1 = 120. Loop it! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.

hints.message: long fact = 1; for(int i=1; i<=n; i++) fact *= i;
Use long not int! 13! overflows int. Start at 1, multiply each i.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Power Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Calculate base^exponent without Math.pow. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Exponents are just repeated multiplication! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.

hints.message: long result=1; for(int i=0; i<exp; i++) result *= base;
Multiply base by itself exp times. Start result at 1 (not 0!).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Reverse a String
practice_exercises.description: Reverse a string using a backward for loop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop backwards through the characters! This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: for(int i=s.length()-1; i>=0; i--) print(s.charAt(i));
Start at last index (length-1), go down to 0.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Count Digits
practice_exercises.description: Count digits in a number using a loop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: How many digits? Keep dividing by 10! You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: int count=0, temp=n; while(temp>0){count++;temp/=10;}
Each /10 removes a digit: 12345->1234->123->12->1->0. Five steps!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: FizzBuzz Loop
practice_exercises.description: Classic FizzBuzz from 1 to 30. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The famous FizzBuzz challenge with a for loop! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1
2
Fizz
4
Buzz
Fizz
7
8
Fizz
Buzz
11
Fizz
13
14
FizzBuzz
16
17
Fizz
19
Buzz
Fizz
22
23
Fizz
Buzz
26
Fizz
28
29
FizzBuzz

hints.message: Check %15 first (both 3 and 5), then %3, then %5.
for(int i=1;i<=30;i++) with if/else if chain.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Find Array Maximum
practice_exercises.description: Find the largest value in an array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The max tracker pattern -- one of the most useful! Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.

hints.message: int max=arr[0]; then loop from 1: if(arr[i]>max) max=arr[i];
Initialise to first element (not 0!). Compare each, keep biggest.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Array Average
practice_exercises.description: Calculate the average of array elements. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sum then divide -- accumulator + division! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: double sum=0; for-each to add. avg = sum / grades.length;
Cast not needed here since grades is already double[].

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: while -- Count Down
practice_exercises.description: Countdown from 5 using while. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: while loop basics -- count down to launch! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: int n=5; while(n>=1){println(n);n--;}
Do not forget n--! Without it, infinite loop.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: while -- Halve Until 1
practice_exercises.description: Halve a number until it reaches 1. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep halving -- the loop ends when n hits 1! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!

hints.message: while(n>=1){print(n+" ");n/=2;}
while(n>=1){System.out.print(n+" ");n/=2;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: while -- Digit Sum
practice_exercises.description: Sum all digits of a number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Extract each digit and add it up! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.

hints.message: int sum=0,t=n; while(t>0){sum+=t%10;t/=10;}
%10 gets last digit, /10 removes it. Repeat until 0.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: while -- Guess Game Sim
practice_exercises.description: Simulate guessing until correct. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep guessing until you get it right! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attempt 1: guessed 3 - too low
Attempt 2: guessed 9 - too high
Attempt 3: guessed 5 - too low
Attempt 4: guessed 7 - correct!

hints.message: while(guess!=target){guess=guesses[attempt];attempt++;...}
Compare: guess<target='too low', guess>target='too high'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: while -- Collatz Conjecture
practice_exercises.description: Apply 3n+1 rule until reaching 1. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Famous maths problem: if even halve, if odd 3n+1. Always reaches 1! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.

hints.message: while(n!=1){print n, if(n%2==0) n/=2 else n=3*n+1, steps++}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: while -- Reverse Number
practice_exercises.description: Reverse digits of a number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build the reversed number digit by digit! This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: int rev=0; while(n>0){rev=rev*10+n%10;n/=10;}
rev*10 shifts left, +n%10 adds last digit. 0->5->54->543->5432->54321.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: while -- GCD (Euclidean)
practice_exercises.description: Find Greatest Common Divisor. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Ancient algorithm by Euclid -- still used today! You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: while(b!=0): swap a=b, b=a%b. When b=0, a is the GCD.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: while -- Binary Conversion
practice_exercises.description: Convert decimal to binary. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Divide by 2, collect remainders -- that is binary! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?

hints.message: String bin=""; while(n>0){bin=n%2+bin;n/=2;}
%2 gives each bit (0 or 1), /2 shifts right. Prepend to build string.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Fix Infinite Loop
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix the loop that never ends. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Classic bug: the loop never stops! Can you fix it? Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.

hints.message: The loop body never changes i. It stays at 1 forever. Add i++;
while(i<=10){println(i);i++;} -- i++ makes it eventually reach 11.

int i=1;
while(i<=10){System.out.println(i);i++;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: while -- Powers of 2
practice_exercises.description: Print powers of 2 up to 1024. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Double each time until you pass the limit! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: int n=1; while(n<=1024){print n; n*=2;}
*=2 doubles each time. 1,2,4,8,16...1024. Stops when next would be 2048.

int n=1;
while(n<=1024){System.out.print(n+" ");n*=2;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: do-while -- At Least Once
practice_exercises.description: Prove body runs even when condition is false. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The do-while ALWAYS runs at least once -- let us prove it! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: do{println(n);n++;}while(n<10); Body runs, THEN checks 100<10 -> false -> exit.
Even though 100 is NOT less than 10, the body ran once.
do{System.out.println(n);n++;}while(n<10);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: do-while -- Menu Loop
practice_exercises.description: Show menu until user quits. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Menus MUST show at least once -- perfect for do-while! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Menu: 1.Play 2.Score 3.Quit
> Score: 100
Menu: 1.Play 2.Score 3.Quit
> Playing...
Menu: 1.Play 2.Score 3.Quit
> Goodbye!

hints.message: do{ show menu; choice=choices[idx++]; switch(choice)... }while(choice!=3);
Menu shows 3 times. Choices: 2(Score), 1(Play), 3(Quit).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: do-while -- Input Validation
practice_exercises.description: Validate input range, retry until valid. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep asking until the input is acceptable! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Input -1: Invalid (1-100)
Input 200: Invalid (1-100)
Input 50: Accepted!

hints.message: do{val=inputs[idx++];if(val<1||val>100)println invalid;}while(val<1||val>100);
First -1(invalid), then 200(invalid), then 50(valid).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: do-while -- Sum Until Zero
practice_exercises.description: Add numbers until user enters 0. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sentinel pattern: keep going until the stop signal! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Added 10, sum=10
Added 25, sum=35
Added 15, sum=50
Total: 50

hints.message: do{n=nums[idx++]; if(n!=0){sum+=n;println...}}while(n!=0);
0 is the sentinel -- signals 'stop'. Sum: 10+25+15=50.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: do-while -- Password Retry
practice_exercises.description: Allow 3 attempts to enter correct password. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine do-while with an attempt counter for security! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attempt 1: wrong - INCORRECT
Attempt 2: nope - INCORRECT
Attempt 3: secret - CORRECT!

hints.message: do{attempt++;check tries[idx++]; if correct success=true;}while(!success&&attempt<3);
Stops when correct OR 3 attempts used.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Compare while vs do-while
practice_exercises.description: Show the difference when condition is initially false. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Side by side: what happens when the condition starts false? This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: while checks first: 100<10? No -> skip. do-while runs first: print 100, then check.
This IS the key difference. while may run 0 times. do-while always runs 1+.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: break -- Find First Negative
practice_exercises.description: Find first negative in array and stop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Search pattern: find it, announce it, break! You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){if(arr[i]<0){print;break;}}
Checks 5,3,8(positive), -2(NEGATIVE!) -> print index 3, break. Never sees -5.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: continue -- Skip Zeros
practice_exercises.description: Sum array but skip zero values. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: continue lets you skip unwanted values! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?

hints.message: if(data[i]==0) continue; sum+=data[i];
Zeros skipped: 5+3+7+2+4=21. continue jumps to next iteration.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: break -- Exit on Threshold
practice_exercises.description: Sum until total exceeds 100. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Keep adding until you pass the limit -- then stop! Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Added 3 items, sum=75
Item 4 would exceed: 75+40=115. Stopped.

hints.message: for each val: if(sum+val>100) break; else sum+=val; count++;
25+30+20=75. Adding 40 would make 115>100 -> break.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: continue -- Only Process Positives
practice_exercises.description: Process only positive numbers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Filter with continue: skip negatives, process positives! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: if(n<=0) continue; then process the positive number.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: break in Nested Loops
practice_exercises.description: Exit only the inner loop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: break exits the INNERMOST loop. Outer keeps going! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: Inner loop: if(j==3) break; Only exits inner. Outer continues.
Each row prints 1,2 then breaks at j=3. Three rows total.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Labeled break -- Exit Both Loops
practice_exercises.description: Use labeled break to exit outer loop. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes you need to break out of TWO loops at once! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!

hints.message: outer: for(i...) for(j...) if(grid[i][j]==target) { print; break outer; }
Label 'outer:' before outer loop. 'break outer;' exits both loops.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: continue -- Print Non-Vowels
practice_exercises.description: Print only consonants from a string. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Skip vowels, print everything else! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.
practice_exercises.expected_output: H l l W r l d

hints.message: if("aeiouAEIOU ".indexOf(ch)>=0) continue;
Check if char is vowel or space, skip if so.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: break vs continue -- Side by Side
practice_exercises.description: Compare break and continue with the same data. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: See the difference: break STOPS, continue SKIPS! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.

hints.message: break: prints 1,2 then exits at 3. continue: prints 1,2, skips 3, prints 4,5.
break = EXIT loop. continue = SKIP iteration.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Nested -- Rectangle of Stars
practice_exercises.description: Print a 4x6 rectangle of stars. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The simplest nested loop: rows and columns! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.

hints.message: Outer: for(i=1;i<=4;i++). Inner: for(j=1;j<=6;j++) print('* '). After inner: println().
4 rows x 6 columns = 24 stars total.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Right Triangle
practice_exercises.description: Print a growing triangle of stars. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Inner loop bound depends on outer variable -- that makes the pattern! This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: for(i=1;i<=5;i++){ for(j=1;j<=i;j++) print('*'); println(); }
Row 1: 1 star. Row 2: 2 stars. j<=i means inner runs i times per row.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Inverted Triangle
practice_exercises.description: Print a shrinking triangle. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Flip it! Start with 5 stars and decrease. You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: Inner: for(j=1; j<=6-i; j++) or for(j=i; j<=5; j++).
Row i has (6-i) or (5-i+1) stars.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Number Triangle
practice_exercises.description: Print numbers instead of stars. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Replace stars with the column number! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?

hints.message: Inner prints j, not stars. for(j=1;j<=i;j++) print(j).
Row 3: j goes 1,2,3. Row 5: j goes 1,2,3,4,5.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Multiplication Grid
practice_exercises.description: Print a 5x5 multiplication table. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The classic times table grid! Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.

hints.message: printf("%3d", i*j) pads numbers to 3 chars for alignment.
%3d means at least 3 characters, right-aligned. 1 becomes '  1'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Diamond Pattern
practice_exercises.description: Print a diamond shape. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Diamonds combine growing and shrinking triangles! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: Each row: (n-i) spaces then (2*i-1) stars. Top: i=1 to n. Bottom: i=n-1 to 1.
Row i: spaces=(n-i), stars=(2i-1). Row 1: 4 spaces, 1 star. Row 5: 0 spaces, 9 stars.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Checkerboard
practice_exercises.description: Print a checkerboard pattern. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Black and white squares alternate based on position! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: if((row+col)%2==0) print #, else print '.'.
Row+col even = black(#), odd = white(.). Alternating pattern.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Nested -- Find Pair Sum
practice_exercises.description: Find two numbers that add to target. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Try every pair -- classic nested loop search! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!

hints.message: for(i=0;i<len;i++) for(j=i+1;j<len;j++) if(arr[i]+arr[j]==target)...
j starts at i+1 to avoid duplicates. 2+7=9 found!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Letter Pyramid
practice_exercises.description: Print letters in a pyramid. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A to E in a pyramid shape -- combining chars and loops! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A
A B
A B C
A B C D
A B C D E

hints.message: Inner: for(char c='A'; c<'A'+i; c++) print(c).
char 'A'+0='A', 'A'+1='B', etc. Row 3: A B C.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Bordered Rectangle
practice_exercises.description: Print a rectangle with border only. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Stars on edges, spaces inside! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.

hints.message: if(row==1||row==rows||col==1||col==cols) star, else space.
First/last row: all stars. Middle rows: star + spaces + star.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Print Prime Numbers
practice_exercises.description: Find all primes from 2 to 50. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A prime has no divisors except 1 and itself. Nested loop to check! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.

hints.message: For each n: loop j from 2 to sqrt(n). If any j divides n, not prime.
Optimisation: only check up to sqrt(n). If n%j==0 found, break and skip.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Spiral Number Grid
practice_exercises.description: Fill a grid spiralling outward. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Advanced challenge: fill a grid in a spiral pattern! This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: Four directions: fill right (top row), down (right col), left (bottom row), up (left col). Shrink boundaries.
top++ after filling right, right-- after down, bot-- after left, left++ after up.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: For-Each -- Word Counter
practice_exercises.description: Count words in a sentence. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Split + for-each = instant word processing! You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: String[] words = s.split(" "); then words.length.
split(" ") breaks at spaces. Array length = word count.

String[] w=s.split(" ");
System.out.println("Words: "+w.length);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Running Average
practice_exercises.description: Calculate running average after each new value. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Update the average with each new data point! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
After 10: avg=10.0
After 20: avg=15.0
After 30: avg=20.0
After 40: avg=25.0
After 50: avg=30.0

hints.message: int sum=0; for(i=0;i<len;i++){sum+=vals[i]; avg=sum/(i+1.0);}
Divide sum by count-so-far (i+1, cast to double).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Palindrome Checker
practice_exercises.description: Check if a string reads the same backwards. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compare first with last, second with second-last... loop! Take your time -- loops click differently for everyone. Some need to trace on paper, others need to run the code.

hints.message: for(int i=0;i<s.length()/2;i++) if(s.charAt(i)!=s.charAt(s.length()-1-i)) not palindrome.
Compare index 0 with last, 1 with second-last, etc. If all match: palindrome.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Bubble Sort
practice_exercises.description: Sort an array using nested loops. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The simplest sorting algorithm -- compare and swap neighbours! If stuck, trace iteration by iteration: what is i? Is the condition true? What happens in the body?

hints.message: Outer: n-1 passes. Inner: compare arr[j] with arr[j+1], swap if wrong.
Each pass bubbles the largest unsorted element to its position.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Count Character Frequency
practice_exercises.description: Count how many times each letter appears. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Frequency counting -- a loop inside a loop! Making mistakes with loops is completely normal. Each error teaches you something about how repetition works.

hints.message: Use int[26] for counts. For each char: counts[ch-'a']++.
ch-'a' converts a=0, b=1, ..., z=25. Increment that slot.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Number Pattern -- Hourglass
practice_exercises.description: Print an hourglass of numbers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Shrink then grow -- like turning a triangle upside down! You have already learned conditions -- loops are just conditions that repeat. You have the foundation!

hints.message: Top: start from (1..n), shrink by 1 each side. Bottom: reverse.
Top half: i rows, each has fewer numbers. Bottom mirrors top.

for(int i=0;i<n;i+=2){for(int s=0;s<i/2;s++)System.out.print(" ");for(int j=i/2+1;j<=n-i/2;j++)System.out.print(j+" ");System.out.println();}
for(int i=n-3;i>=0;i-=2){for(int s=0;s<i/2;s++)System.out.print(" ");for(int j=i/2+1;j<=n-i/2;j++)System.out.print(j+" ");System.out.println();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Array Rotation
practice_exercises.description: Rotate array left by k positions. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Shift every element left. Wrap the first to the end! Professional developers write loops every single day. This is a core skill you are building right now.

hints.message: Simple: create new array, new[i] = old[(i+k)%len].
(i+k)%len wraps around. i=0: (0+2)%5=2 -> arr[2]=3.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Two Sum Search
practice_exercises.description: Find if any two numbers sum to target. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Classic coding challenge: find the pair! Focus on getting the loop bounds right first. The body logic is usually the easy part.

hints.message: Nested loop: for each pair (i,j where j>i) check sum.
2+7=9. Found! Break both loops.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Remove Duplicates
practice_exercises.description: Find unique values in array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Check if each element appeared before -- nested loop comparison! If the output looks wrong, add a print inside the loop to see what is happening at each step.

hints.message: For each element, check all previous: if found, skip (continue).
For arr[i], loop j=0 to i-1. If arr[j]==arr[i], it is a duplicate.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Simple Encryption Shift
practice_exercises.description: Caesar cipher: shift letters by n. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Encrypt text by shifting each letter -- loops + char arithmetic! This exercise builds on patterns you have already seen. Look for the familiar structure.

hints.message: For each char: char c = (char)('A' + (ch - 'A' + shift) % 26);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Matrix Transpose
practice_exercises.description: Transpose rows and columns of a 2D array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Swap rows and columns: element [i][j] goes to [j][i]! You are doing brilliantly. Loops are one of the hardest concepts and you are tackling them head-on.

hints.message: int[][] t = new int[cols][rows]; t[j][i] = m[i][j];
2x3 becomes 3x2. [0][1]=2 goes to [1][0]=2.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Game of Life Step
practice_exercises.description: One step of Conway's Game of Life. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections. Think about what the loop variable does at each step.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The ultimate nested loop challenge: simulate cellular automata! Think of the loop as a recipe: what do you do first? What do you repeat? When do you stop?

hints.message: For each cell: count 8 neighbours. Apply birth/survival rules. Write to NEW grid.
Count neighbours with nested i-1..i+1, j-1..j+1 loop (skip self, check bounds).
